coredata.dc:title: Photothermal/NO combination therapy from plasmonic hybrid nanotherapeutics against breast cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Vayalakkara, Reesha Kakkadavath
Lo, Chun-Liang
Chen, Hsin-Hung
Shen, Ming-Yin
Yang, Ying-Chieh
Sabu, Arjun
Huang, Yu-Feng
Chiu, Hsin-Cheng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a plasmon-semiconductor nanotheranostic system comprising Au nanostars/graphene quantum dots (AuS/QD) hybrid nanoparticles loaded with BNN6 and surface modified with PEG-pyrene was developed for the photo-triggered hyperthermia effect and NO production as the dual modality treatment against orthotopic triple-negative breast cancer. The structure and morphology of the hybrid nanodevice was characterized and the NIR-II induced thermal response and NO production was determined. The hybrid nanodevice has shown enhanced plasmonic energy transfer from localized surface plasmonic resonance of Au nanostars to QD semiconductor that activates the BNN6 species loaded on QD surfaces, leading to the effective NO production and the gas therapy in addition to the photothermal response. The increased accumulation of the NIR-II-responsive hybrid nanotheranostic in tumor via the enhanced permeation and retention effects was confirmed by both in vivo fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging. The prominent therapeutic efficacy of the photothermal/NO combination therapy from the BNN6-loaded AuS@QD nanodevice with the NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm against 4T1 breast cancer was observed both in vitro and in vivo. The NO therapy for the cancer treatment was evidenced with the increased cellular nitrosative and oxidative stress, nitration of tyrosine residues of mitochondrial proteins, vessel eradication and cell apoptosis. The efficacy of the photothermal treatment was corroborated directly by severe tissue thermal ablation and tumor growth inhibition. The NIR-II triggered thermal/NO combination therapy along with the photoacoustic imaging-guided therapeutic accumulation in tumor shows prominent effect to fully inhibit tumor growth and validates the promising strategy developed in this study.

In vitro cellular uptake and therapeutic effect
In vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging and biodistribution
In vivo therapeutic effect from NIR-II mediated combination therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the NO/photothermal combination therapy with photoacoustic imaging property of the plasmon-exciton hybrid nanotheranostics (AuS@QDBNPEG NPs) triggered by the NIR-II irradiation giving a promising cancer treatment efficacy. An electron transfer from plasmon to exciton that activates BNN6 loaded on QD surfaces and mediates NO release.

a) TEM image of AuS@QD hybrid NPs (scale bar: 200 nm). b) HR-TEM image of AuS@QD hybrid NPs (scale bar: 10 nm). c) XPS of Au 4f peaks of AuS@QD NPs and Au nanostars. d,e) Deconvoluted XPS C 1s and O 1s peaks of AuS@QD NPs. f) Raman spectra of AuS@QD NPs and graphene QDs. g) Fluorescence emission spectra of QDs and AuS@QD NPs. h) TEM elemental (Au and C) mapping of AuS@QD NPs (scale bar: 100 nm).

a) TEM image of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs (scale bar: 100 nm). b) Absorbance spectra of AuS@QD, AuS@QDBNPEG and AuS@QDPEG NPs. c) Temperature elevation curves of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs treated with NIR-II laser at 1064 nm (1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min at different concentrations. d) Corresponding thermal images of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs. e) The concentration of NO released from free BNN6, AuS@QDPEG NPs and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs under NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm, determined by Griess assay. NAC was used an NO scavenger. f) Generation of NO from AuS@QDBNPEG NPs treated with NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W cm−2) with different irradiation durations (mean ± SD, n = 3). Samples were analyzed with one-way ANOVA, followed by POSTHOC Tukey for multiple comparisons *P < 0.05, **P < 0.005.

a) DAF-FM fluorescence images of 4T1 cells showing intracellular NO generation after receiving different treatments. NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm) was conducted at the power density of 1.0 W cm−2 for 5 min. Hoechst 33342 was used to identify cell nuclei. b) Quantified DAF-FM fluorescence intensity of 4T1 cancer cells (mean ± SD n ≥ 6) (scale bar: 100 μm). c) Intracellular DAF-FM fluorescence intensity histograms by flow cytometric analysis after 4T1 cells being treated with various NPs (mean ± SD n = 3). One-way ANOVA, followed by POSTHOC Tukey for multiple comparisons used for statistical analysis. *P < 0.05.

a) In vitro therapeutic effect of NPs under hyperthermia, NO and combination therapy (1064 nm, 1 W cm−2, 5 min) against 4T1 breast cancer cells by MTT assay (mean ± SD n = 6). b) Fluorescence images of necrotic 4T1 cells via Hoechst 33342/PI double staining. Corresponding Quantified data of the PI fluorescence intensity represented as IOD (mean ± SD n = 4) are included. Samples were analyzed with one-way ANOVA, followed by POSTHOC Tukey for multiple comparisons. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.005 and ***P < 0.0005.

a) In vitro PA imaging contrast of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and the linearity of the PA values with the NP concentration. b) The in vivo PA images of tumors in tumor-bearing mice at different time points after intravenous injection of NPs and the corresponding change of PA signal intensity with time (mean ± SD n = 3). c) PA images of tumors in oxy-hemoglobin mode at different time points and the corresponding quantification of oxygen saturation in tumor with time (mean ± SD n = 3).

a) In vivo biodistribution of DiI labeled AuS@QDPEG and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in orthotropic 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. DiI was used as a fluorescence probe for the IVIS measurement. The white circles indicate the tumor sites at 24, 30 and 48 h after intravenous injection of NPs. b) Ex vivo images of major organs and tumors isolated at 24 h post-injection (mean ± SD n = 3). Quantified data of the relative fluorescence intensity are also included (mean ± SD n = 3).

In vivo therapeutic evaluation of various treatments against orthotropic 4T1 breast cancer (mean ± SD n = 6). a) The in vivo temperature elevation curves of tumors receiving different NP and NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W cm−2, 5 min) treatments. b) Corresponding thermal IR images of tumors. c) The tumor growth curves of 4T1 tumors with different treatments. d) Photos of tumors harvested at day 22 after the first treatment. e) Tumor weights with mice being sacrificed at day 22. f) H&E examination of tumor tissue sections harvested from the tumor-bearing mice at the end point of the treatments (scale bar: 20 μm). **P < 0.005 compared the samples by one-way ANOVA with POSTHOC Tukey test.

a) Fluorescence images of tumor sections harvested from the tumor-bearing mice receiving different treatments. The tumor sections were stained with Hoechst 33342 to identify cell nuclei (blue) and IHC stained with 3-nitrotyrosine marker. The corresponding IOD values of the nitrotyrosine marker in tumors receiving various treatments are included. b) IHC staining of caspase-3 in tumor sections and the corresponding IOD values from the caspase 3 marker (scale bars: 20 μm) in different treated groups (mean ± SD, n ≥ 6). Samples were analyzed by POSTHOC Tukey method for multiple comparisons in one-way ANOVA. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.005. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Photothermal/NO combination therapy from plasmonic hybrid nanotherapeutics against breast cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a plasmon-semiconductor nanotheranostic system comprising Au nanostars/graphene quantum dots (AuS/QD) hybrid nanoparticles loaded with BNN6 and surface modified with PEG-pyrene was developed for the photo-triggered hyperthermia effect and NO production as the dual modality treatment against orthotopic triple-negative breast cancer. The structure and morphology of the hybrid nanodevice was characterized and the NIR-II induced thermal response and NO production was determined. The hybrid nanodevice has shown enhanced plasmonic energy transfer from localized surface plasmonic resonance of Au nanostars to QD semiconductor that activates the BNN6 species loaded on QD surfaces, leading to the effective NO production and the gas therapy in addition to the photothermal response. The increased accumulation of the NIR-II-responsive hybrid nanotheranostic in tumor via the enhanced permeation and retention effects was confirmed by both in vivo fluorescence and photoacoustic imaging. The prominent therapeutic efficacy of the photothermal/NO combination therapy from the BNN6-loaded AuS@QD nanodevice with the NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm against 4T1 breast cancer was observed both in vitro and in vivo. The NO therapy for the cancer treatment was evidenced with the increased cellular nitrosative and oxidative stress, nitration of tyrosine residues of mitochondrial proteins, vessel eradication and cell apoptosis. The efficacy of the photothermal treatment was corroborated directly by severe tissue thermal ablation and tumor growth inhibition. The NIR-II triggered thermal/NO combination therapy along with the photoacoustic imaging-guided therapeutic accumulation in tumor shows prominent effect to fully inhibit tumor growth and validates the promising strategy developed in this study.

NIR-II activated photothermal/NO combination therapy from plasmonic heterostructured nanotherapeutics against triple-negative breast cancer.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) is a vital signaling gasomediator involved in a wide range of physiological and pathological processes [1,2]. NO is thought to play a multifaceted role in pro- or anti-tumorigenesis, angiogenesis, metastasis and immune responses, depending on its concentration at the target site. For example, NO, in the picomolar range, promotes tumor progression by conferring enhanced proliferative and angiogenic properties to cancer cells [3,4]. By contrast, a high concentration (≥ 1 μM) of NO can be useful in suppressing tumor cell growth, retarding cancer metastasis, reversing multi-drug resistance and sensitizing tumor cells to chemotherapy, radiotherapy and immunotherapy [5]. NO has also been exploited as a vascular mediator to augment the enhanced permeability and retention effect in tumors, thereby improving the intratumoral delivery of nanomedicines [6,7]. Despite of the above-mentioned progress in NO therapy against malignant neoplasm, the short half-life and chemical instability only allow NO to diffuse over limited distance, which significantly hinders its full therapeutic potential [8]. Many efforts have been devoted to the development of stimuli-responsive systems to induce NO release in a spatiotemporally controllable manner to maximize the therapeutic effect of NO gas therapy [9].

In this context, a number of small molecules acting as NO donors such as diazeniumdiolates (NONOates), S-nitrosothiols (RSNOs), and bis-N-nitroso compounds (BNNs) have been developed for on-demand NO release [10,11]. The release of NO from donor molecules can be triggered by endogenous or external factors including pH changes, transition metal ions (Cu+), light, heat, or enzyme activity [12,13]. Among these stimuli, light irradiation is capable of accurate external modulation of the site, allowing a therapeutic action with high temporal and spatial resolution. Near-infrared (NIR) laser radiation, in particular the NIR second region (NIR-II, 1000‐1700 nm), which features good penetration depth and minimally invasive characteristics has received widespread attentions [14]. Unfortunately, NO release is only responsive to ultraviolet (UV)/visible (Vis) light from the donors such as RSNOs and BNNs. Therefore, nanoplatforms, which can respond sensitively to NIR light, but effectively avoid premature cargo leakage, is conducive to achieve site-specific controlled NO release with enhanced biosafety.

Recently, a variety of NIR absorption nanomaterials have been explored for targeted NO delivery [14,15]. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) capable of converting NIR to high energy UV/Vis light through a multiphoton process have been adopted into the photo-activated system to release NO [16]. However, practical applications of UCNPs suffered from low UC quantum yield, resulting in insufficient NO production and unsatisfactory therapeutic outcomes. NIR-mediated NO release can also be achieved by locally heating thermo-labile NO-donors, depending on the in-situ heat generated by photothermal agents including Prussian blue NPs [6], bismuth sulfide NPs [15], polydopamine NPs [17,18], and MXenes [14]. However, the release of NO usually requires sufficient heat dosage to generate a relatively high temperature (e.g., 60 °C). It remains challenging due to the inevitable injury to surrounding normal tissues under high irradiation power, particularly in the first NIR biological window (NIRI, 750–1000 nm) [15]. In 2015, Fan et al. demonstrated for the first time that graphene oxide can transform NIR photons into active electrons, leading to the decomposition of BNNs into NO [19]. Meanwhile, numerous efforts have thus far been made to enhance the light-graphene interaction using plasmonic metal NPs [20,21]. Noble metal NPs capable of triggering localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) have been introduced to mediate the efficient light collection and subsequent plasmon-enhanced excitation of intrinsic graphene electrons [21]. The hot electrons energized due to the LSPR excitation in the plasmonic NPs can also be injected into the nearby graphene structure to augment the overall photoresponsivity. Therefore, the design of metal-semiconductor heterojunctions is expected to extend the function of controlled NO release with the combined effect of photothermal heating and strong exciton-plasma coupling.

In this study, gold nanostars (AuS) decorated with graphene quantum dots (QD) were adopted as a promising plasmon-exciton hybrid nanotheranostics to achieve photoacoustic imaging (PAI)-guided photothermal/NO combination therapy (Fig. 1 ). Au nanostars, possessing multiple sharp spikes, display exceptional field enhancement of plasmon modes in both NIR-I and NIR-II (1000–1700 nm) biological windows [22]. The NIR-II featuring increased tissue penetration and maximum permission energy (≥1.0 W cm−2) for skin, is emerging as a more preferable light source for PAI and PTT [23]. Au nanostars can also be used to serve as an effective NIR-II “antenna” to promote the interfacial energy/charge transfer process, thereby enhancing the capability of QDs acting as a semiconductor to form excitons with electron-hole pair formation. In addition, the accommodation of QDs on AuS surfaces can also improve the stability of a colloidal dispersion and provide an ultrahigh surface area for increasing the loading of the NO donors, BNN6, and the polymeric stabilizer, PEG-pyrene. Under NIR-II irradiation, AuS@QDBNPEG NPs exhibit desirable PA contrast enhancement and PTT performance. On-demand NO release can also be achieved with high photochemical efficiency (Fig. 1), attributing to the enhanced direct electron transfer from LSPR of Au nanostars to QDs that activates the NO donor and induces the NO generation. Interestingly, burst release of NO not only leads to excess production of oxidative/nitrosative stress, but also accelerates glutathione oxidation simultaneously. The disruption of cellular redox balance ultimately results in a significantly enhanced PTT/NO antitumor activity. Therefore, the hybrid nanostructures featuring strong plasmon–exciton interactions would serve as an ideal nanotheranostics to achieve photonic-triggered thermo/gaseous therapy toward imaging-guided synergistic oncotherapy.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Alfa Aesar (USA). l -Ascorbic acid was acquired from Avantor (USA). Sodium citrate was provided by Showa (Japan). Methoxy-poly(ethylene glycol)-NH2 (mPEG-NH2; Mw 2000 g/mol) was supplied by JenKem Technology (Beijing, China). 1-Pyrenebutanoic acid succinimidyl ester, citric acid, N,N′-di-sec-butyl-1,4-phenylenediamine (BPA), 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), N-acetyl cysteine (NAC) and 1,1′-dioctadecyl-3,3,3′,3′-tetramethylindocarbocyanine perchlorate (DiI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NaNO2 was obtained from Duksan Pure Chemicals. Griess reagent for the NO assay was provided by Promega (USA). Intracellular NO probe, DAF-FM diacetate, intracellular glutathione detection kit (Thioltracker Violet), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), RPMI-1640 medium, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% trypsin–EDTA solution, penicillin-streptomycin solution, and Hoechst 33342 were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Dialysis membrane with MWCO 1000 was provided by Orange Scientific (Belgium). Murine 4T1 mammary carcinoma cell line was obtained from the Food Industry Research and Development Institute (Hsinchu City, Taiwan). For animal experiment studies, Clinical Chemistry Reagent Disc was obtained from Amishield (Taiwan). Anti-caspase antibody and anti-rabbit IgG antibody Texas red were purchased from Arigobiolaboratories (Taiwan). The secondary antibody Alexa Fluor 488 was provided by Invitrogen (USA). Anti-mouse CD31 antibody was obtained from Bioscience (USA). Anti-3-nitrotyrosine antibody purchased from Merck (USA) was used for NO staining on tumor tissues. Deionized water was produced from Milli-Q synthesis (18 MΩ, Millipore).

Preparation and characterization of the graphene quantum dot used in this work was conducted as reported previously [24] and the detailed description was included in the Supplementary Materials (SM). AuS@QD hybrid NPs adopted in this work were obtained first by the synthesis of gold seeds, followed by the preparation of Au nanostars from Au seeds while graphene QDs were added and adsorbed on the surfaces of the nanostars simultaneously, leading to the formation of AuS@QD NPs. For the preparation of Au seeds, the aqueous solution of sodium citrate (2.2 mM, 75 mL) was heated up to 100 °C for 1 h under refluxing with vigorous stirring. HAuCl4 (25 mM, 0.5 mL) was then added into the solution and [25–27] the reaction proceeded for ca 15 min with a significant color change of the reaction solution from yellow to cherry pink, indicating the formation of Au seeds in solution. The plamon-exciton hybrid AuS@QD NPs were prepared by a surfactant-free Au seed-mediated growth approach. To an aqueous HAuCl4 solution (10 mL, 0.25 mM) under acidic conditions with stirring, Au seeds were first added, followed by addition of AgNO3 (1 mM, 100 μL) and ascorbic acid (100 mM, 50 μL). Immediately, the previously prepared QD (400 mg mL−1, 40 μL) was added and the reaction pH was neutralized with 1.0 N NaOH solution. The reaction was conducted at 25 °C for 24 h. The AuS@QD NPs were formed as indicated by a color changes from pale yellow to deep blue. The NPs were washed with DI water twice and collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 25 min. For the preparation of pristine AuS, the same procedures were conducted, yet without the addition of QDs.

Synthesis of BNN6 as an NO donor was carried out as reported elsewhere [19]. The synthetic route and structural characterization were described in SM. For BNN6 loading, to an aqueous AuS@QD dispersion solution (1 mg, 1 mL), BNN6 in DMSO (1 mg, 200 μL) was added dropwise. The mixture was stirred at room temperature in dark for 24 h. Afterward, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 25 min and the pellets containing the AuS@QDBN NPs were collected and re-suspended in DI water. For the surface modification process, PEG-pyrene (2 mg mL−1 in DI water, PEG MW 2000 g/mol) was added into the aqueous suspension of AuS@QDBN NPs and the mixture was kept stirring over 24 h in dark. The AuS@QDBNPEG NPs were purified by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 25 min. The NPs collected were re-dispersed in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, I 0.01 M) and stored at 4 °C for further use. As a control sample, BNN6-free AuS@QDPEG NPs were prepared in a similar manner, but without BNN6 loading. The synthesis and structural confirmation of the PEG-pyrene adduct was conducted as described in SM. The morphological and structural characterizations of the AuS@QDPEG NPs and, with the BNN6 loading, the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs were described in SM. The loading efficiency and loading content of BNN6 within the plasmonic heterostructured AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was evaluated and the colloidal stability in different cell culture media, the in situ photothermal performance and the NO production of the NPs upon the NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm were determined as described in SM.

Murine mammary 4T1 cancer cell line was adopted in this study and the cells were cultured in DMEM (1% pyruvate, 10% FBS, 100 units mL−1 penicillin, and 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin) in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. For the cellular uptake study, AuS@QDBNPEG NPs were labeled with DiI via hydrophobic association for fluorescence detection. 4T1 cells (2.5× 105 cells per well) were seeded onto 22 mm glass coverslip in 6-well plates for 24 h. The cells were then washed with PBS and the NP samples (50 μg mL−1) in DMEM were added. The incubation reaction proceeded for prescribed time intervals and the cells were washed with PBS three times, and fixed with 4% formaldehyde. The cells were stained with Hoechst 33342 and F-actin staining KIT-Green Fluorescence-Cytopainter (Abcam). The cellular uptake of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was evaluated on an LSCM (Zeiss LSM 780, Germany) with the fluorescence channels for DiI (λex 535 nm and λem 585 nm), Hoechst channel, and F-actin marker (FITC channel). Quantitative determination was performed based upon the mean integrated optical density (IOD) of fluorescence intensity of ten randomly selected fields determined by Image Pro Plus software. The level of cellular uptake of the NPs by 4T1 cells was also determined quantitatively by the measurement of the DiI fluorescence intensity after cellular NP uptake and cell lysis with DMSO on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

NIR-II-induced NO production from intracellular AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in 4T1 cells was evaluated with the intracellular NO fluorescent probe, DAF-FM diacetate. AuS@QDPEG NPs were also adopted as a negative control. With 4T1 cells being co-incubated with AuS@QDBNPEG (100 μg mL−1) for 24 h and washed thoroughly, the cells were incubated with DAF-FM diacetate in fresh medium (10 μM) for 1 h before irradiation. The cells were laser irradiated at 1064 nm (1 W cm−2) for 5 min. After irradiation, the cells were washed and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and the nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33342. Fluorescence images were captured with optical microscope (Olympus IX70) equipped with CCD under Hoechst and FITC channels. The intracellular DAF-FM (λex 480 nm and λem 515 nm) level was quantitatively determined by Image Pro Plus software from a minimum of 5 microscopic fields. The quantified fluorescence intensity was presented as the mean integrated optical density (IOD). The intracellular NO release was further examined by flow cytometric measurements. 4T1 cells were cultured in 6-well plates (1 × 106 cells) for 24 h and the NPs (150 μg mL−1) were added for further incubation over 24 h. DAF-FM was added and the reaction proceeded for 1 h prior to the 5-min NIR-II irradiation. The cells were washed and re-suspended in PBS and the fluorescence intensity from DAF-FM was analyzed on an FACS Calibur flow cytometer (BD Bioscience) using the FITC channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro combined effect of photothermal/NO therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the NPs without NIR-II activation was evaluated by MTT assay. 4T1 cancer cells (1 × 103 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates in DMEM (200 μL) and cultured for 24 h. The cells were co-incubated separately with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and AuS@QDPEG NPs in the concentration range 25–150 μg mL−1 for 24 h. The medium was removed and the cells were rinsed with PBS and re-cultured in DMEM. The MTT reagent in PBS (0.25 mg mL−1) was added to each well, followed by 3-h incubation at 37 °C. After removal of culture medium, DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan precipitate and the absorbance of the DMSO solution at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader (SpectraMax M5, Molecular Devices, USA). Cell viability was evaluated as the ratio of the absorbance of treated cells to the absorbance of control. The in vitro therapeutic effect of the combination therapy against 4T1 cancer cells was examined by MTT assay and PI/Hoechst 33342 double staining, respectively. 4T1 cells (1× 105) were seeded in a 24-well plate in DMEM at 37 °C for 24 h. Co-incubation of the cells with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and AuS@QDPEG NPs separately at prescribed concentrations was carried out for 24 h in DMEM. Free BNN6 at the equivalent drug concentrations to AuS@QDBNPEG was adopted as a control group. After being washed with PBS and trypsinized, the cells in DMEM (40 μL) was irradiated with 1064 nm laser (1.0 Wcm−2) for 5 min. The temperature was monitored by the IR thermal camera. Following the NIR-II irradiation, cells were resuspended in 200 μL DMEM and seeded onto the 96-well plates for 36 h. The cell viability was evaluated by MTT assay. To verify the NO effect alone against 4T1 cells, the experiment was repeated yet with the irradiation at 1064 nm being carried out at 4 °C. By contrast, the hyperthermia ablation effect on 4T1 cells was demonstrated with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs being irradiated in the presence of NAC (10 mM) as an NO scavenger. The in vitro combined therapeutic effect of NPs was also examined with PI/Hoechst 33342 staining. With the cells being treated with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs for 24 h and NIR-II irradiation at 1064 nm for 5 min, the cells were stained with Hoechst 33342 and PI (both 0.005 mg mL−1) and examined on a Nikon Eclipse TiU inverted microscope equipped with both Hoechst set (λex 360 nm, λem 461 nm) and PI channel set (λex 493 nm, λem 637 nm). Free BNN6 and AuS@QDPEG NPs were used as control samples. In addition, the effect of NIR-II irradiation treatment of intracellular AuS@QDBNPEG NPs on GSH depletion was evaluated as described in SM.

The linearity of the in vitro photoacoustic intensity versus the concentration of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was established in the concentration range 0.31–5.00 mg mL−1. The in vivo photoacoustic imaging was carried out with BALB/C tumor-bearing mice. The in vivo animal studies in this work were conducted on six-week-old BALB/C female mice purchased from the National Laboratory Animal Center, Taiwan. The use of laboratory animals was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan (approved number: IACUC 10343). Approved guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals from IACUC were followed throughout the study. The 4T1 breast cancer cells (1 × 106 cells mL−1, 100 μL PBS) were injected into the mammary fat pad beneath the fourth mammary gland in mice. The hair was removed. With the tumor volume reaching 200 mm3 calculated as V = L × W2/2, where L and W are the tumor dimensions at the longest and shortest points, respectively, the mice were intravenously injected with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs (100 μL, Au: 20 mg kg −1). Photoacoustic mice images were acquired using a Vevo LAZR-X photoacoustic imaging system (FUJIFILM VisualSonics) at 0, 2, 8, 24, and 48 h post-injection with mice being anesthetized with 2% isoflurane throughout the experiments. The laser irradiation was conducted at 808 nm at the power density of 1.0 W cm−2 for 2 min. The in vivo biodistribution of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs within tumor-bearing mice after intravenous administration and the ex vivo accumulation of the NPs in tumor and major organs upon the mice sacrifice was evaluated by the In Vivo Imaging System (IVIS) using DiI as a fluorescence probe.

The mice were randomly divided into 5 groups including PBS+L, free BNN6+L, AuS@QDBNPEG, AuS@QDBNPEG+L, AuS@QDPEG+L groups with 6 mice in each group (n = 6). As aforementioned, the orthotopic breast cancer was established in mice by inoculation of 4T1 cancer cells into the fat pad beneath the abdominal mammary glands. Mice bearing the tumors with the size of ca 200 mm3 were injected twice intravenously with NPs or free BNN6 at a BNN6 dose of 5 mg kg−1 (equivalent to the Au dose of 20 mg kg−1 for the BNN6-free NPs) at day 0 and day 3, respectively. The tumor was then treated with NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm (1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min at 24 h after each injection. The temperature was recorded every 30 s and the thermal images were captured every minute during the 5-min irradiation with an IR thermal camera. The tumor size and the body weight of the treated mice were monitored every other day after the irradiation treatment over 22 days. The mice were sacrificed at day 22 after the treatment and the tumor and spleen were harvested and weighed.

The production of highly reactive nitrogen species derived from NO in tumor tissues, in particular the peroxynitrite and nitrogen dioxide species, leads to the nitration of tyrosine residues in tissue proteins. The extent of protein nitrotyrosine formation representing as a measure of the production of reactive nitrogen species and potential cell damage was evaluated with IHC staining using rabbit anti-nitrotyrosine antibody as the primary antibody and goat anti-rabbit IgG FITC as the secondary antibody. To identify cell apoptosis in tumor tissues, rabbit anti-caspase 3 antibody and goat anti-rabbit IgG Texas red were used as the primary and secondary antibody, respectively. Similarly, the blood vessels in tumor tissues were identified by IHC staining, using rat anti-mouse CD31 primary antibody and Alexa Fluor 488 conjugated goat anti-rat secondary antibody. Cell nuclei were stained with Hoechst 33342. The fluorescence images were captured on a Nikon ECLIPSE TI-U inverted microscope (Japan) equipped with fluorescence channels for Hoechst 33342, Texas red, FITC (Alexa Fluor 488). Randomly captured 10 images were analyzed by Image-Pro Plus software and the fluorescence intensity was quantified and noted as IOD. For the IHC staining of 3-nitrotyrosine and caspase 3, the tumors were harvested from mice sacrificed at day 6 after the two treatments while the IHC staining of CD31 was performed on the isolated tumor tissues at day 22. The evaluation of the tissue GSH (reduced form) level was carried out by the staining of tumor tissue with Thioltracker™ Violet. The tumor was harvested at day 6 after the two treatments and sliced into tissue sections of ca 10 μm in thickness. The thiol tracker (20 mM) was incubated with tumor tissue for 30 min at 37 °C, followed by PI (0.005 mg mL−1) staining for 10 min. The tissues were fixed and examined under fluorescence microscopy. The systemic toxicity of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in tumor-bearing mice was evaluated in terms of the H&E staining of major organ tissues and blood biochemical parameters. The detailed description in experimental procedures was included in SM.

A one-way ANOVA test was adopted for all the data analyses using SPSS software, followed by POSTHOC Tukey for multiple comparisons. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.005 and ***P < 0.0005. Error bars represent mean ± SD.

To develop an effective NIR-II responsive plasmonic heterostructured nanotheranostics, the graphene QDs serving as a semiconductor were first prepared by the direct citric acid pyrolysis approach according to the method previously reported [24]. The QDs were characterized by UV/Vis, fluorescence emission and FTIR, respectively. The results shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Material, SM) provided supportive evidences to confirm the chemical structure of graphene QDs. The plasmonic star-shaped Au NPs were prepared by the seed-mediated method meanwhile the gold seeds were synthesized by the reduction of HAuCl4 with sodium citrate in aqueous phase under refluxing and vigorous stirring [25]. The Au seeds were characterized by TEM, SEM and UV/Vis spectroscopy. The results illustrate that the aqueous Au seed dispersion appeared in soft pink color (Fig. S2a) while the TEM and SEM examination revealed that these colloidal NPs are rather monodispersed and spherical in morphology (Fig. S2b and c). The Au seeds showed the maximum absorbance occurring at 540 nm in their UV/Vis spectrum (Fig. S2d) and a Dh of 16.24 ± 0.98 nm evaluated by DLS (Table S1). Being distinct from the synthesis of Au nanostars previously reported [22], the graphene QD-decorated Au nanostars (AuS@QD) were prepared in a one-pot manner first by a new facile seed-mediated approach in which the reduction of HAuCl4 with ascorbic acid serving as the reducing agent led to the growth of Au seeds into Au NPs. The NPs were formed in star shape due to the presence of silver nitrate (AgNO3), being consistent with the data reported elsewhere [26]. Owing to the absence of surfactant in the Au nanostar preparation, the absorption of graphene QDs on the surfaces of Au nanostars occurring at the neutral pH in the second step was highly enhanced meanwhile the absorbed QDs concomitantly rendered the AuS@QDs of the excellent dispersion in aqueous phase [27]. It is also expected that the direct contact of AuS with graphene QDs without the spatial segregation from surfactant inevitably enhances the plasmon-exciton resonance energy transfer from Au nanostars to QDs, which mediates the BNN6 activation and the NO production. The Au NPs in star shape, attained from the first-step reaction as aforementioned was verified by SEM (Fig. S3a). Nevertheless, due to the absence of pertinent surfactant to improve the colloidal stability, aggregation of the bare Au NPs in aqua occurred within 20 min (Fig. S3b). By contrast, with the AuS being decorated with QDs on their surfaces, the AuS@QDs were found well dispersed in aqueous medium (Fig. S3c) obviously because of the abundant presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups in graphene QDs [26,27]. The TEM image shown in Fig. 2a confirms the star shape of the AuS@QDs with a mean size of ca 150 nm in diameter, in consistence with the Dh value (152 nm) of the NPs from the DLS measurements (Table S1). The deposition of QDs on Au nanostars was further corroborated by the HR-TEM examination, showing the graphene QD layer on the surfaces of Au nanostars with the thickness of ca 10 nm (Fig. 2b) [28,29].

The surface analysis of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for chemical species revealed that AuS@QDs are comprised of 54.47 wt% C, 41.46 wt% O, and 4.67 wt% Au (Table S2). The data clearly indicate the formation of AuS@QDs used to serve as a plasmon-exciton heterostructured system for energy transfer enhancement. Owing to the absence of surfactant in the Au nanostar preparation, the XPS analysis of chemical composition shows 100 wt% Au in bare AuS (Table S2). It is worthy to mention that the QDs can not only act as a stabilizer for the improved dispersion of AuS@QDs in aqua, but also provide loci for BNN6 loading and its activation in the plasmon-exciton hybrid system [30]. With the formation of the AuS@QD heterostructured NP system, the XPS peaks (Fig. 2c) of Au 4f orbital for AuS@QD were observed at 83.4 eV and 87.1 eV which are attributed to the Au0 reduction state. A slight negative shift in the binding energy of the AuS@QD NPs compared to the bulk value of the bare Au nanostars (84. eV) was caused presumably by small fluctuations in electronic structure during the hybrid NP formation (Fig. 2c) [31]. In addition to the peaks for Au 4f, the peaks at 335 eV and 354 eV were assigned to Au 4d as shown in Fig. S4a [32] together with the characteristic peaks of C 1s and O 1s of QDs at ca 284 eV and 531 eV in accordance with those attained from bare QDs [33]. Further deconvolution of the C 1s peaks from the AuS@QD sample confirms the peaks for CC, C−O and CO at 284.1 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d). Similarly, the deconvolution of the O 1s peaks of the AuS@QD sample in Fig. S4a gave rise to the peaks belonging to CO (531.0 eV) and C-O-C (532.8 eV) (Fig. 2e). For comparison, the XPS survey scan spectrum of bare QDs and the corresponding deconvoluted peaks are included in Fig. S4 [33,34]. The AuS@QD hybrid NPs were also characterized by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2f, both bare QD and AuS@QD samples exhibit characteristic peaks at 1353 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1 in the Raman spectra known as the typical D and G bands for carbon-based materials. It is noted that these two bands from the QD sample became broader and less intensive than those from the AuS@QD NPs because of the increased dissipation caused by the fluorescence emission of QDs compared to AuS@QDs. Fig. 2g shows the fluorescence emission spectra of QDs and AuS@QD NPs of the same QD concentration in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) with the excitation at 375 nm. The data clearly indicate that the fluorescence emission of QDs with being deposited on the AuS surfaces was appreciably quenched most probably due to the close packing into dense layers on the AuS surfaces. The close contact of Au nanostars with QDs is a prerequisite for effective light conversion into energy flow from metal plasmon to QD excitons for subsequent BNN6 activation. The TEM images and elemental mapping of the AuS@QD NPs are shown in Fig. 2h. The data also confirmed the AuS@QD NPs in star shape with the presences of two major elements (C and Au) in the hybrid NP system developed in this work. The SEM image and elemental mapping (Fig. S5) showed the results fully consistent with the observations from the TEM examination.

The NIR-II responsive NP system was equipped with the NO production by loading of BNN6 serving as an NO donor into the hybrid therapeutic system. BNN6 was synthesized by the reaction of BPA with sodium nitrite (NaNO2) as reported elsewhere [19]. The chemical structure and composition of BNN6 were confirmed by 1H NMR, UV/Vis and FTIR spectroscopy and elemental analysis (Fig. S6 and S7 and Table S3). Considering the needs to improve the colloidal stability and prolong the circulation time in vivo of the hybrid nanotheranostics, the surfaces of drug loaded AuS@QD (AuS@QDBN) NPs were modified with PEG-pyrene conjugate, taking advantage of the accessibility of pyrene to π-π stacking with the QDs and/or BNN6 on NP surfaces. The PEG-pyrene conjugate was synthesized by the reaction of mPEG-NH2 (MW 2000 g/mol) with 1-pyrenebutanoic acid succinimidyl ester [35]. The PEG-pyrene structure was confirmed with 1H NMR and the fluorescence property of the polymer-pyrene conjugate was examined with the fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S8). The conjugation efficient was ca 90%. Both the loading of BNN6 and the coating of PEG-pyrene onto the 2D structured QD semiconductors was achieved primarily by π–π stacking owing to the characteristic aromatic structures of the two payloads. Similar observation on the effective immobilization of 1-pyrenebutanoic acid succinimidyl ester onto single-walled carbon nanotubes via π-π stacking has been reported previously [36]. The hydrophobic effect might also facilitate in part the association of the payloads with NPs. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S9a) of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs illustrates the featured bands of BNN6 at 1451 cm−1 (N-N=O) and 1080 cm−1 (CN stretching) and of PEG-pyrene at 1616 cm−1 and 1680 cm−1 from the amide I and II carbonyl groups and at 1009 cm−1 from C-O-C stretching (PEG), indicating the presences of both BNN6 and PEG-pyrene on AuS@QDBNPEG NPs. The fluorescence spectra in Fig. S9b demonstrate the significant fluorescence quenching of the PEG-pyrene adduct with being absorbed on NP surfaces as compared to that of the free PEG-pyrene adduct [35]. The loading content of BNN6 in NPs was evaluated by the determination of the BNN6 concentration with the absorption measurement at 370 nm of the supernatant after the centrifugation of the AuS@QDBNPEG solution in DMSO. The loading content and the loading efficiency were ca 30 wt% and 40%, respectively. Obviously, the high loading content was attributed to effective π–π interaction and hydrophobic association between graphene QDs and BNN6, similarly to the previous report that described the strong interactions of BNN6 with graphene oxide for effective entrapment of BNN6 in the sandwich graphene oxide structure [19]. A high level of the BNN6 entrapment into the hybrid NPs also ensures the sufficient production of NO for the substantial cytotoxic effect against breast cancer in this study.

The TEM image of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs showed in Fig. 3a indicates that the NPs retain the nanostar shape, similar to the AuS@QD NPs (Fig. 2a) though the NPs were loaded with BNN6 and modified with PEG-pyrene on surfaces. The mean size of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs based upon the TEM examination was ca 156 nm in diameter while the DLS data showed the Dh value of 176 nm (Table S1 and Fig. S10). The latter is slightly larger in size most likely due to the extended PEG chain segments on the NP surfaces in aqueous phase. Fig. 3b shows the absorbance spectra of AuS@QD, AuS@QDPEG and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs with the emphasis on the significant absorption occurring in the NIR-II region (>1000 nm). It has been known that the plasmon absorption of Au NPs occurs in a strong shape-dependent manner. The Au NPs in star (multi-pod) and echinus shapes are typical examples to exhibit the plasmon absorption not only in the NIR-I but also NIR-II region [37]. The AuS@QD NPs in this work retain the plasmon absorption in both the NIR-I and II regions albeit being modified on the surfaces with PEG-pyrene and BNN6. The absorption is one of the essential factors operating for generating NIR-II induced hyperthermia response and energy transfer from LSPR that activates the loaded BNN6 species for NO production in addition to its photoacoustic contrast in imaging applications [38]. The NIR-II responsive agents have been receiving increasing interests in the past decade in therapeutic and bioimaging applications due to the improved tissue penetration of NIR-II light compared to UV/Vis and NIR-I counterparts [39]. Nevertheless, the data shown in Fig. 3b strongly suggest that the star-shaped AuS@QDBNPEG NPs exhibit eminent photothermal property as well as photoacoustic contrast in both NIR-I and -II spectral regions, which might broaden their biomedical applications via their combination uses for enhanced theranostic effects [40]. The colloidal stability of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was examined in terms of the time-evolved size variation of NPs in cell culture media, i.e., DMEM and RPMI. As shown in Fig. S11, the particles remained essentially identical size in both media over 48 h, manifesting the excellent colloidal stability caused appreciably by the stabilization effect of PEG chain segments modified on NP surfaces. The zeta potential values of graphene QDs and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs are summarized in Fig. S12. The negative value of the QDs in zeta potential confirms the occurrence of partial oxidation of the QDs that leads to the production of the COOH groups and renders the AuS@QD NPs of the negative zeta potential and the hydrophilic propensity on surfaces. With accommodating BNN6 and PEG-pyrene, the resulting AuS@QDBNPEG NPs became almost neutral in zeta potential (ca −2 mV). This is ascribed primarily to the steric exclusion effect of the PEG chains on NP surfaces. The photothermal effect of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was examined by the irradiation of the aqueous NP dispersion with NIR-II laser at 1064 nm (1.0 W cm−2). The temperature rise profiles are shown in Fig. 3c. The temperature of the aqueous NP dispersion at the concentration of 150 μg mL−1 was increased by 39.3 °C in 10 min while the temperature increase was only ca 9 °C for DI water. The photothermal effect was found to be strongly dependent on the NP concentration up to 150 μg mL−1, suggesting excellent NIR-II-mediated hyperthermia behavior. The corresponding photothermal images captured by the IR thermal imaging camera (Fig. 3d) also confirms the capability of the synthesized AuS@QDBNPEG NPs to undergo plasmon heating reaction in response to NIR-II irradiation. The photothermal capability of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was examined with the NIR-II irradiation treatment in a repeatedly ON/OFF manner for every interval of 5 min over 1 h (1.0 W cm−2). The results shown in Fig. S13a demonstrate the superior structural persistence of the NPs to undergo repeated NIR-II-mediated plasmonic heating without significant decay in both heating rate and amplitude. This is further supported by the essentially unchanged absorption profiles of the NPs after receiving the irradiation treatment for various durations up to 30 min (Fig. S13b). The TEM image (Fig. S13c) showed negligible changes in morphology after their exposure to NIR-II laser for 30 min, in consistence with the responses of the NPs in temperature changes and absorption profiles to the repeated irradiation treatment.

The NO production via plasmon-semiconductor energy transfer of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was examined upon the irradiation of the NPs with NIR-II laser at 1064 nm. The NO concentration was evaluated by the Griess assay. The Griess assay determines the concentration of nitrite ions (NO2 −) as one of two primary stable and nonvolatile breakdown products of NO after its exposure to aqueous medium. While the NO production was barely observed from free BNN6 with irradiation at 1064 nm, the NO species was appreciably generated with the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs being NIR-II laser irradiated (Fig. 3e). As the negative control groups, the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs either in the absence of NIR-II activation or the presence of NAC serving as an NO scavenger were unable to effectively produce NO. In contrast to little response of BNN6 to the irradiation at 1064 nm (Fig. 3e), the NO production was observed with free BNN6 being irradiated at 312 nm for 40 min, the extent being strongly dependent upon the BNN6 concentration (Fig. S14a). Nevertheless, comparing with free BNN6 at the same concentrations, the NO release from the BNN6 species loaded within the AuS@QDBNPEG NP system with irradiation at 312 nm became obviously more pronounced. This is ascribed to the limited solubility of free BNN6 in aqueous phase, leading to severe aggregation of the free drug and its limited photo-activation. For precise comparison, the NO production upon the treatments of free BNN6 and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs, respectively, with the NIR-II irradiation at 1064 nm at the same power density as that with at 312 nm was also included as Fig. S14b. The BNN6 activation and the NO production can thus be attained via the irradiation either at 312 nm on BNN6 (both free and encapsulated) or at 1064 nm only with the BNN6 species being loaded on the AuS@QDBNPEG NP system. While the former takes place by the UV light absorption that directly induces the structural decomposition of BNN6, the latter plausibly involves the effects of high temperature and enhanced energy transfer on the BNN6 breakdown with the NIR irradiation. In addition to the photothermal effect the energy transfer from LSPR may occur either via re-emission as scattered photons or directly to the semiconductors closely associated. Three mechanisms have been identified for the plasmonic energy transfer enhancement from LSPR to semiconductors, namely photonic enhancement effect, plasmonic induced resonance energy transfer and direct electron transfer [41,42]. The former two processes require an essential overlap in the absorption band of the semiconductor with the LSPR resonance band. Since little absorption was observed for the graphene QDs serving as the semiconductor at 1064 nm, the energy transfer can only take place by direct electron transfer of hot electrons contained in LSPR to the QDs in the conduction band (CB as shown in Fig. 1), which induces the charge separation and thus the electron/hole pair formation in the semiconductor. The loaded BNN6 species upon the NIR-II irradiation of the plasmonic heterostructured NPs was thus activated for the NO release. By contrast, with the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in aqueous solution being heated to 60 °C in water bath, little NO release was detected. The observation is accordance with the data previously reported [19]. Though the temperature effect on the BNN6 activation may not be entirely excluded, it is rather evident that, in this work, the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs are capable of undergoing response in the NO production to the NIR-II irradiation primarily by the plasmonic energy transfer enhancement from LSPR of Au nanostars to QDs operative with the direct electron transfer pathway. The significant NO production amounting to the concentration of 60 μM from the AuS@QDBNPEG NP sample at the concentration of 100 μg mL−1 upon laser irradiation was observed compared to only 6.9 μM NO for the sample in the absence of laser irradiation (Fig. 3e). Fig. 3f shows the dependence of the NO production from the irradiation treatment of the NPs on the BNN6 concentration and NIR-II irradiation time. With the irradiation being prolonged from 1 min to 5 min, the effect of the BNN6 concentration on NO production becomes more pronounced, indicating an excellent BNN6 loading capacity within the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs, which thus requires prolonged irradiation duration to release all NO species covalently encaged within the BNN6 donor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and therapeutic effect
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of AuS@QDBNPEG by murine 4T1 breast cancer cells was evaluated, respectively, by the fluorescence image examination of the treated cells with laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) and the spectrometric determination of the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence probe after lysis of the treated cells with DMSO. DiI was used to label AuS@QDBNPEG NPs by hydrophobic effect as a fluorescence probe. The data are shown in Fig. S15. It is obvious that the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs were engulfed by 4T1 murine breast cancer cells and the quantity was increased with the incubation time over 24 h. The LSCM images (Fig. S15) show the location of the NPs within cancer cells mostly in cytoplasm regions. The intracellular NO generation after the uptake of the NPs by the cancer cells in response to NIR-II irradiation was evaluated by staining with DAF-FM diacetate. DAF-FM is often used as an intracellular NO probe which reacts with NO to produce fluorescent benzotriazole for detection [43]. It can be seen in Fig. 4a that, with being irradiated at 1064 nm (1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min, only the cells pre-treated with the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs produced NO significantly as reflected by the fluorescence intensity of the probe. The amount of the NO production from the free BNN6 group is negligible as expected since photoirradiation conducted at 1064 nm is unable to activate free BNN6 for the NO release. The NIR-II irradiation of the AuS@QDPEG NP-pretreated cancer cells also barely produced NO simply because of the void of BNN6. The quantified fluorescence intensity data from the fluorescence microscopic images are illustrated in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity measurement of the photo-irradiated cancer cells after being pretreated with free BNN6 or AuS@QDBNPEG NPs by flow cytometry showed similar results to those from the microscopic examination (Fig. 4c).

Fig. S16 shows that the fluorescence intensity within the cells treated with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was substantially reduced either in the absence of NIR-II activation or the presence of NAC playing a role as the NO scavenger in comparison with the AuS@QDBNPEG group upon the NIR-II activation. The temperature rise profiles of the NP-pretreated 4T1 cells in culture medium in response to NIR-II irradiation treatment are shown in Fig. S17. The temperature elevation was essentially identical between the AuS@QDPEG and AuS@QDBNPEG groups since the LSPR generated upon the NIR-II laser treatment of Au nanostars is the major contribution to the high temperature reaction. The graphene QDs prepared in this work do not exhibit the photothermal property in the NIR region as evidenced by the lack of the absorption in this region. Fig. S17 also illustrates the increase of the temperature with the NP concentration, being in the range 47–56 °C with the NP concentration range 50–150 μg mL−1 after the 5-min NIR-II irradiation.

NO has been found to exhibit several diverse activities in biological systems. It can react with oxygen, superoxide (O2 −) and hydrogen peroxide, leading to the formation of various reactive nitrogen species (RNS) such as NO2 − and ONOO− capable of undergoing oxidation further [44,45]. These RNS are very prone to S-nitrosylation with cysteine-containing peptides and proteins. Glutathione (GSH, reduced form) is one of the most abundant intracellular thiol-containing peptides and plays a key regulator on redox balance. As shown in Fig. 1, the intracellular GSH level can be thus decreased with its S-nitrosylation reaction with NO. To evaluate the effect of NO on the cellular GSH level, 4T1 cancer cells were treated in sequence with AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and NIR-II irradiation, followed by the fluorescence staining with a thiol probe, Thioltracker Violet. The fluorescence images and quantified data from the fluorescence microscopic examination are shown in Fig. S18. Owing to the inability to produce NO, neither AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in the absence of NIR-II irradiation nor the free BNN6 species with being irradiated at 1064 nm was found to deplete GSH intracellularly. Nevertheless, the intracellular GSH level was dramatically decreased with the 4T1 cancer cells, following the uptake of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs, being treated with the NIR-II irradiation. The detailed description of the S-nitrosylation of GSH with the peroxynitrite species that gives rise to GSNO and further oxidation with another GSH to form GSSG is illustrated in Fig. 1 [46]. A similar NO-induced GSH depletion device has been reported with the supramolecular assembly of α-CD-Ce6-NO NPs that liberate NO to oxidize GSH to GSSG in MCF-7 cancer cells [47]. Somewhat surprisingly, the intracellular GSH level of 4T1 cancer cells sequentially treated with BNN6-free AuS@QDPEG NPs and NIR-II irradiation was appreciably decreased. This is ascribed to the generation of electrons/holes in the semiconductor within the plasmonic heterostructured nanosystem as a result of enhanced plasmonic energy transfer that induces charge separation in excitons via the direct electron transfer pathway upon the NIR irradiation of the plasmonic metal/semiconductor nanocomposites. The electrons and holes generated on the surface of the plasmon exciton hybrid nanosystem by laser irradiation have been found capable of acting as a catalyst with strong reductive and oxidative propensity, respectively, as reported elsewhere [48]. In this work, both the hole catalytic activity and the NO species generated upon the NIR-II irradiation of the intracellular AuS@QDBNPEG NPs was obviously leveraged enormously to deplete GSH and thus alter the redox balance in the cancer cells.

The in vitro therapeutic effect of the photothermal/NO combination therapy from the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs against murine 4T1 breast cancer cells was evaluated by MTT assay. Both the AuS@QDPEG and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs up to the concentration of 150 μg mL−1 have shown nontoxic nature toward 4T1 cells in the absence of photo-irradiation treatment (Fig. S19). With effective photothermal effect or NO release or both being triggered, the cell viability of 4T1 cancer cells was reduced significantly, in particular with the combination therapy (Fig. 5a). Appreciable reductions in cell viability were also observed with the 4T1 cancer cells being treated with the AuS@QDPEG NPs and NIR irradiation, in particular at the NP concentrations equal to or greater than 50 μg mL−1. In consistence with the temperature profiles shown in Fig. S17, the results signify the pronounced photothermal effect from the LSPR of the Au nanostars in the plasmonic hybrid NPs on killing cancer cells. The NO effect attained from the treatment of the AuS@QDBNPEG NP group with NIR irradiation at 4 °C on killing cancer cells is similar to that solely from the photothermal treatment. With the photothermal/NO combination therapy being applied on the AuS@QDBNPEG NP group at a BNN6 dose of 45 μg mL−1, the cancer cells in the culture medium were almost killed completely. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence images of the cancer cells with PI staining to identify late apoptosis and necrosis of cancer cells after the treatments. The data illustrate the highest PI fluorescence intensity of the AuS@QDBNPEG NP+L group among all different treatments, signifying the prominent performance of the photothermal/NO combination therapy of the NIR-II- mediated AuS@QDBNPEG NPs to kill cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging and biodistribution
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo animal studies in this work were conducted on six-week-old BALB/C female mice purchased from the National Laboratory Animal Center, Taiwan. The use of laboratory animals was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan (approved number: IACUC 10343). Approved guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals from IACUC were followed throughout the study. To evaluate the tumor accumulation and biodistribution of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in vivo, the mice bearing orthotopic 4T1 breast cancer with the tumor size of ca 200 mm3 were injected intravenously with the NPs. The time-evolved tumor accumulation of the NPs was first examined by PA imaging, taking advantage of the plasmonic resonance property of Au nanostars in the NPs. As shown in Fig. 6a, the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs exhibit excellent optical absorption contrast in the PA signal, the signal intensity being linearly proportional to the NP concentration. This signifies the prominent capability of the NPs to serve as an exogenous contrast agent for PA imaging acquired for the image-guided NP delivery to the target sites. Fig. 6b illustrates the PA images of the orthotopic tumor in mice at different time points after the treatment with either AuS@QDPEG or AuS@QDBNPEG NPs. As shown by the mean PA signal intensity of the NPs in tumor receiving the laser irradiation at 808 nm, the accumulation of both the NPs reached the highest levels at 24 h post-injection and, after that, at the time point of 50 h, the PA intensity was appreciably decreased. This manifests the pertinent time point in terms of the maximal tumor accumulation for the onset of photothermal/NO combination therapy and the clearance/degradation of the NPs in tumor over 50 h. In addition, the measurement of the oxygen level in tumor was performed by the PA signal of oxy-hemoglobin from the tumor-bearing mice after the treatment of AuS@QDPEG or AuS@QDBNPEG NPs. Fig. 6c illustrates a similar pattern in the change of tumor oxygenation with time after the NP treatment. This is ascribed to the photothermal effect upon the irradiation of the AuS@QDBNPEG and AuS@QDPEG NPs in tumor by inducing vessel dilation and increasing permeability in tumor tissues that leads to increased tumor oxygenation. It is readily understood that the increase of the oxygenation level be strongly influenced by the amount of the NPs accumulating in tumor in a proportional manner as illustrated in Fig. 6c.

The in vivo and ex vivo biodistribution study of the NPs after intravenous administration in mice bearing orthotropic 4T1 breast cancer was conducted with IVIS. The NPs were labeled with DiI as a fluorescence probe by hydrophobic association. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . The in vivo fluorescence images of the mice treated respectively with AuS@QDPEG and AuS@QDBNPEG NPs show similar fluorescence intensity in tumor area (highlighted by white circle in Fig. 7 a) at 24 h post-injection at which the signal intensity reached the highest level and maintained relatively the intense signal over 30 h. The observation is in consistence with the data attained with the PA measurements. The fluorescence imaging obtained from the PBS group was caused simply by the autofluorescence of the mice used. The ex vivo fluorescence images of isolated major organs and tumor (Fig. 7 b) showed the appreciable accumulation of the NPs in tumor most likely due to the enhanced permeability and retention effects that facilitate the delivery of nanomedicines in a naturally occurring manner to solid tumor site. Free BNN6 was not used as a control because of its poor aqueous solubility and, more importantly, weak fluorescence emission for in vivo and ex vivo examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effect from NIR-II mediated combination therapy
ce:section.ce:para:
Encouraged by the in vitro therapeutic data against 4T1 cancer cells, the in vivo anti-tumor efficacy was examined with orthotropic 4T1 tumor-bearing BALB/c mice. The mice were separated randomly into the PBS+L, free BNN6+L, AuS@QDBNPEG, AuS@QDPEG+L and AuS@QDBNPEG+L groups with 6 mice in each group. The irradiation treatment referred to as L herein was performed at 1064 nm at the power density of 1.0 W cm−2 for 5 min. Two NP doses were given at day 0 and 3 intravenously at each dosage of 5 mg kg−1 BNN6 and, for the AuS@QDPEG+L group, 20 mg kg−1 Au. The 5-min irradiation treatment was conducted twice in total at 24 h after each injection. The tumor temperature was recorded with an IR imaging thermal camera during the laser irradiation. The NIR-II laser irradiation at 1064 nm was adopted as aforementioned because of its enhanced tissue penetration compared to the NIR-I laser in the region 700–900 nm. The difference in the penetration ability between NIR-I (808 nm) and NIR-II (1064 nm) irradiation through a pork tissue sample (ca 0.5 cm in thickness) was evaluated with the characterization procedures being described in Supplementary Material. Fig. S20 shows that the laser penetration of NIR-II at 1064 nm (1.0 Wcm−2) in terms of the percentage of laser transmission was ca 32% compared to only 22% with the NIR-I laser at 808 nm. The result clearly indicates an approximately 50% increase of the 1064-nm laser in the tissue penetration ability with respect to that of the NIR-I laser at 808 nm primarily due to the reduced photon absorption and scattering with the NIR-II laser [49]. Fig. 8
a and b show the temperature rise profiles and IR thermal images of the mice at the tumor sites irradiated at 24 h after the first NP treatment. The prominent increase of the tumor temperature to ca 55 °C was observed for the mice being treated with either AuS@QDBNPEG or AuS@QDPEG NPs followed by the laser irradiation over 5 min. The hyperthermia reaction in response to the second irradiation on the tumors receiving the second dose of NPs was also monitored. As shown in Fig. S21, the laser irradiation on tumors after the second NP treatment also gave rise to similar temperature profiles. The effect of the photothermal therapy alone on inhibiting tumor growth as shown in Fig. 8c is rather pronounced as the tumor size being reduced from ca 300 to 100 mm3 over 10 days from the onset of the treatment. The mice also showed the burn scars after the treatment as a result of the thermal ablation of tumor tissue (Fig. S22). Nevertheless, the tumor regrew gradually after and the tumor reached the size of ca 430 mm3 at day 22 at which the mice were sacrificed. Fig. 8
d and e show the photo and weight of isolated tumor harvested at day 22, being consistent with the tumor sizes shown in Fig. 8c. By contrast, the free BNN6+L group and the AuS@QDBNPEG group did not show significant difference in tumor growth compared with the PBS+L group, indicating negligible therapeutic action when both NO and photothermal reaction was absent. The therapeutic effect on tumor growth inhibition was further enhanced with the tumor-bearing mice receiving the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs alongside the NIR-II laser irradiation. The tumor size was dramatically reduced to 90 mm3 at day 22 with no sign of tumor regrowth (Fig. 8c). The tumor mass obtained upon the mice sacrifice at day 22 (Fig. 8d and e) also indicates the best therapeutic outcome when the photothermal/NO combination therapy was adopted. The burn scar occurring after the laser irradiation disappeared on day 22, revealing the tissue wound repairing in the tumor area (Fig. S22). As shown in Fig. 8f, the H&E tissue staining of tumors receiving the AuS@QDBNPEG+L treatments showed severe tissue damage primarily as a result of cell necrosis and hemorrhagic inflammation [50] meanwhile the tissue damage was somewhat lessened with the tumor being treated with AuS@QDPEG NPs and laser irradiation in the absence of NO therapy. With only photothermal therapy being applied, the pronounced effect on inhibiting tumor growth was observed (Fig. 8c) mainly due to severe thermal ablation that caused cell necrosis and apoptosis. It is worthy to note that the spleen was obviously enlarged with the mice bearing orthotropic 4T1 breast cancer. The splenomegaly is caused by the leukemoid reactions due to the splenic infiltration of cancer cells. Fig. S23 shows the photo images and mass of spleens harvested from the mice at day 22 after receiving various treatments in comparison with the healthy mice. The spleens of the negative control groups void of the photothermal or NO treatment were obviously enlarged and the spleen mass was increased to ca 7 folds of the healthy counterpart. While the increase of spleen mass (ca 3.3 folds) was appreciably reduced with the mice receiving the AuS@QDPEG+L treatment, an increase less than 50% in spleen mass from the AuS@QDBNPEG+L group with respect to the healthy mice was observed. The results also manifest the prominent therapeutic efficacy from the photothermal/NO combination therapy against orthotropic 4T1 breast cancer in company with effectively reducing the infiltration of cancer cells into spleen.

The therapeutic effects of the photothermal/NO dual modality treatment was further evaluated in terms of the levels of GSH, caspase 3 and nitration of the tyrosine residues (3-nitrotyrosine) of cellular proteins by fluorescence staining. Being consistent with the observation on the in vitro GSH depletion in 4T1 cancer cells by the treatment of AuS@QDBNPEG NPs with NIR-II-irradiation, the AuS@QDBNPEG+L group showed the highest GSH depletion in tumors among all various treatments. This is ascribed primarily to the NO-induced GSH oxidation along with the catalysis on GSH oxidation of the holes generated from the enhanced plasmonic energy transfer to the excitons in the hybrid nanosystem upon the NIR-II irradiation (Fig. S24). The catalytic effect of the generated holes of the activated QD excitons on GSH oxidation can be seen from the appreciably reduced fluorescence intensity of the thiol tracker (Thioltracker Violet) in tumors receiving the AuS@QDPEG NP+L treatment (in the absence of NO production). It is anticipated that the originally elevated oxidative stress of cancer cells compared to normal cells is plausibly further enhanced by the GSH depletion with the nanotherapeutic action. Furthermore, the levels of 3-nitrotyrosine in tumors receiving different treatments were also evaluated with the IHC tissue staining. The 3-nitrotyrosine residues in proteins are attained by the nitration reaction of the tyrosine residues with the peroxynitrite species. The peroxynitrite species are produced via a diffusion-driven reaction of NO with radical superoxide anion (O2∙−) in mitochondria although NO is usually generated outside mitochondria. With the tyrosine residues of mitochondrial proteins being nitrated, the protein structure and functions are thus altered and disrupted [51]. The quantity of the 3-nitrotyrosine of mitochondrial proteins are thus often adopted to serve as a measure of the NO production and therapeutic efficacy. Fig. 9a shows the appreciably enhanced fluorescence intensity of 3-nitrotyrosine with IHC staining on tumor tissues receiving AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and NIR-II laser irradiation treatment. In addition to the effective NO production, the results signify the prominent performance of the NO species to generate peroxynitrite-mediated nitrosative stress in tumor microenvironment. As expected, negligible protein tyrosine nitration was observed in tumor tissues of the free BNN6+L, AuS@QDBNPEG and AuS@QDPEG+L groups because of the absence of the NO production. With the increases of the nitrosative and oxidative stress as aforementioned, the cell apoptosis was evaluated in terms of the level of caspase 3 expression examined by the IHC staining. The results are shown in Fig. 9b. The cell apoptosis was found appreciably enhanced in tumor tissues receiving the photothermal therapy from the AuS@QDPEG+L treatment as compared to the untreated group. The cell apoptosis was even further enhanced in the tumor tissues of the AuS@QDBNPEG+L groups, thereby signifying the additional effect from the NO therapy to kill tumor cells. It has been recognized that the NO-mediated cell death occurs primarily with high NO concentrations that induce increased cellular oxidative and nitrosative stress and cause cell damage and apoptosis [52]. With a low concentration, the NO species can modulate vessel normalization and tissue oxygenation meanwhile, at a high dose, it shows strong antiangiogenic response and vessel eradication effect [53,54]. Fig. S25 shows the fluorescence images of tumor tissue sections with IHC staining on CD31 as a marker of tumor blood vessels in addition to the staining with Hoechst 33342 to identify cell nuclei. As expected, with the hyperthermia treatment being applied in the AuS@QDPEG+L groups, the blood vessels were considerably eradicated owing to severe thermal tissue ablation. The blood vessels were found to be even more destroyed with the tumor being treated with the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs and NIR-II irradiation. This is mainly ascribed to the anti-angiogenic effect of NO at the sufficiently high concentrations [5,55].

The biosafety of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs in tumor-bearing mice was studied in terms of the time-evolved body weight change, the blood biochemistry assays and H&E histologic examination of major organs. Fig. S26a shows the insignificant variation in body weight of the mice receiving different treatments, including the AuS@QDBNPEG and AuS@QDPEG NPs with and without NIR-II irradiation. The results strongly illustrate the absence of severe acute toxicity induced by the AuS@QDBNPEG and AuS@QDPEG NPs alongside the laser irradiation. The data from the blood biochemistry study with the emphasis on the functions of liver and kidney show insignificant differences in all the values of liver/kidney function markers in comparison with those from healthy mice (Fig. S26b). The histological examination of organ tissues by H&E staining showed no apparent organ damage, indicating the excellent biosafety with the NP doses adopted by intravenous administration in this study (Fig. S26c).

In this study, a new NIR-II responsive plasmonic heterostructured nanodevice as a multi-functional hybrid nanotheranostics was developed for breast cancer treatment. The heterostructured nanodevice was comprised of Au nanostars and graphene QDs to play the roles, respectively, as the plasmon and the semiconductor as a BNN6-loaded composite carrier to achieve photothermal response and NIR-II-induced plasmonic energy transfer enhancement mainly via direct electron transfer pathway for activation of the NO donor. The structure, morphology and photo-triggered responsivity of the plasmonic heterostructured nanotheranostics in the hyperthermia reaction and the NO production were well characterized. The photothermal/NO combination therapy by NIR-II activation of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs showed prominent therapeutic efficacy against 4T1 breast cancer both in vivo and in vitro. This NIR-II-responsive NO release hybrid nanosystem could provide an on-demand NO therapy in combination with PTT for effective local treatment on tumor through the photoactivation process controlled by external NIR-II irradiation, giving rise to excellent in vivo curative effect in inhibiting the growth of orthotopic breast cancer. The plasmonic resonance property of the Au nanostars in the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs also impart the device the prominent photoacoustic imaging contrast when used in vivo. The biosafety study, body weight measurements and H&E staining of major organ tissues signified the bioinert and nontoxic nature of the AuS@QDBNPEG NPs when being used intravenously at the dose range adopted.
